dev_plans.content: Lightweight heuristic implementation for subgroup fairness monitoring. **What is measured**: Per-epoch TPR (sensitivity) and FPR across the 6 ethnicity groups on the validation set, then the maximum absolute pairwise gap between any two groups (equalized-odds style gap). **Where in pipeline**: Inserted as a callback at the end of every training epoch, right after the standard validation loss/accuracy computation. **Implementation steps**: (1) Ensure the validation DataLoader yields a tuple `(x, y, ethnicity_id)` where `ethnicity_id` is an integer 0–5; if the loader is built from a pandas DataFrame, carry the ethnicity column through a custom `collate_fn`. (2) Create a `FairnessCallback` class with an `on_validation_epoch_end` hook. Inside, run model inference on the validation set in eval mode, collect predicted probabilities, true labels, and ethnicity IDs into three numpy arrays. (3) Apply a fixed decision threshold of 0.5 to get binary predictions. (4) For each of the 6 groups, compute a 2×2 confusion matrix with `sklearn.metrics.confusion_matrix`; derive TPR = TP/(TP+FN) and FPR = FP/(FP+TN). Skip groups with fewer than 20 samples to avoid degenerate ratios, logging a warning. (5) Compute `eo_gap = max_i_j |TPR_i - TPR_j|` and `fpr_gap = max_i_j |FPR_i - FPR_j|`; take the larger of the two as the headline number. (6) Log per-group TPR/FPR plus the gap to TensorBoard (scalar tags `fairness/tpr_group_{k}`, `fairness/eo_gap`) and append a CSV row `epoch,eo_gap,fpr_gap,tpr_0,...,tpr_5` to `logs/fairness_heuristic.csv`. **Output artifacts**: a TensorBoard scalar, the CSV timeseries, and at the end of training a matplotlib line chart `eo_gap_over_epochs.png` with a horizontal dashed line at the threshold. Trivially fast (adds <1s per epoch) and requires only numpy + scikit-learn.
dev_plans.metric: Maximum pairwise equalized-odds gap across the 6 ethnicity groups per epoch, defined as max(max_ij|TPR_i - TPR_j|, max_ij|FPR_i - FPR_j|) on the validation set at threshold 0.5.
dev_plans.threshold: 0.15 — the equalized-odds gap should stay below 15 percentage points. Published MIMIC/CXR fairness audits (Seyyed-Kalantari 2021, Röösli 2022) report per-group TPR/FPR gaps in the 8–20% range for well-trained models on imbalanced health datasets; a 5% target is aspirational and rarely crossed without explicit debiasing, while 0.15 is reachable by a vanilla, well-tuned training run and still flags genuinely problematic disparities.

dev_plans.content: Statistically rigorous implementation with bootstrap confidence intervals and multi-metric aggregation. **What is measured**: A comprehensive per-ethnicity panel of AUROC, AUPRC, sensitivity, specificity, PPV, NPV, FPR, FNR, and Brier score, plus equalized-odds gap, demographic-parity difference, and predictive-parity difference, each with 1000-resample bootstrap 95% CIs. The single headline scalar is the bootstrap-mean equalized-odds gap with significance flagging when the lower CI bound exceeds zero. **Where in pipeline**: Runs every N epochs (default N=1 for small models, N=5 for large ones since 1000 bootstraps over 6 groups can take 10–60s) as a `FairnessEvaluator` object invoked from the training loop after validation. Also runs once post-training on the held-out test set for a final report. **Implementation steps**: (1) Subclass the validation Dataset to return the ethnicity attribute alongside (x, y). (2) Build `FairnessEvaluator.evaluate(probs, labels, groups)` that first computes point estimates for all 9 per-group metrics via scikit-learn. (3) Bootstrap: for b in 1..1000, draw a stratified resample within each group (preserves group sizes), recompute the per-group metrics and the three group-fairness gaps, and store results in a (1000, n_metrics, n_groups) array. Use `np.random.default_rng(seed=epoch)` for reproducibility and `joblib.Parallel(n_jobs=-1)` to parallelize. (4) Compute 2.5/97.5 percentiles for CIs; compute pairwise CI overlap matrices. (5) Pick the bootstrap mean of the equalized-odds gap as the per-epoch scalar and emit it to TensorBoard/W&B; serialize the full panel (point estimates, CIs, gap distributions) to `logs/fairness_epoch{E}.json`. (6) At end of training, generate a violin plot of bootstrap gap distributions per epoch and a forest plot of per-group metric CIs. Include a significance flag when the lower bound of the EO-gap CI exceeds the threshold. **Output artifacts**: per-epoch JSON panels, a headline scalar timeseries, a final PDF report with forest plots and violin plots, and a pytest-style assertion at training end that raises if the final EO-gap lower CI exceeds the threshold. Requires numpy, scikit-learn, scipy, joblib, matplotlib.
dev_plans.metric: Bootstrap mean of the equalized-odds gap across 6 ethnicity groups per epoch, computed from 1000 stratified resamples on the validation set.
dev_plans.threshold: 0.10 — a 10-percentage-point gap in the bootstrap-mean EO gap. Bootstrap means are typically slightly tighter than single-point estimates because of averaging, so this is set below the heuristic 0.15 but still above the aspirational 0.05 overlapping-CI target from the probe description; this matches the typical residual disparity after standard regularization in MIMIC-style multi-ethnic cohorts and is achievable by a well-tuned baseline.

dev_plans.content: Tooling/library-based implementation using Fairlearn and Aequitas. **What is measured**: The Fairlearn `MetricFrame` computes per-group AUROC, selection rate, TPR, FPR, precision, recall, and Brier score; `fairlearn.metrics.equalized_odds_difference`, `demographic_parity_difference`, and Aequitas disparity ratios produce the aggregate fairness scalars. The headline metric is Fairlearn's `equalized_odds_difference` on the validation set per epoch. **Where in pipeline**: Wrapped as a Lightning `Callback` (or plain Keras `Callback`) invoked at `on_validation_end`. Also exposed as a standalone `python -m fairness.audit --ckpt ...` CLI for post-hoc audits on arbitrary checkpoints. **Implementation steps**: (1) `pip install fairlearn aequitas` and pin versions (fairlearn>=0.10, aequitas>=1.0). (2) Extend the validation dataset to surface an `ethnicity` series (pandas categorical with 6 levels). (3) In the callback's hook, run inference to produce `y_pred_proba` and `y_pred` (threshold 0.5), stored as numpy arrays; wrap ethnicity into a `pd.Series` aligned to the prediction index. (4) Build `MetricFrame(metrics={'auroc': roc_auc_score, 'tpr': true_positive_rate, 'fpr': false_positive_rate, 'precision': precision_score, 'brier': brier_score_loss}, y_true=y_true, y_pred=y_pred_proba, sensitive_features=ethnicity)`; log `mf.by_group` as a table to W&B and extract `mf.difference(method='between_groups')` for each metric. (5) Call `equalized_odds_difference(y_true, y_pred, sensitive_features=ethnicity)` and `demographic_parity_difference(...)` for the aggregate scalars. (6) Pass the same arrays through Aequitas `Group().get_crosstabs()` + `Bias().get_disparity_predefined_groups()` with a configurable reference group (default: largest group) to obtain disparity ratios and a disparity heatmap. (7) Log the scalar EO difference to TensorBoard/W&B as `fairness/eod`; store per-epoch Fairlearn MetricFrame and Aequitas disparity DataFrames as parquet under `artifacts/fairness/epoch={E}`. (8) At end of training, render Fairlearn's built-in `plot_model_comparison` and Aequitas's `Fairness().plot_disparity()` into a single HTML dashboard served via `mkdocs` or written to `artifacts/fairness/report.html`. **Output artifacts**: TensorBoard/W&B scalar timeseries, per-epoch parquet tables, and an interactive HTML fairness dashboard. Leverages battle-tested libraries so reviewers can trust the metric definitions; lowest implementation risk.
dev_plans.metric: Fairlearn equalized_odds_difference (max of TPR-difference and FPR-difference between any two of the 6 ethnicity groups) on the validation set per epoch.
dev_plans.threshold: 0.12 — Fairlearn's equalized_odds_difference should remain below 0.12. Fairlearn's definition matches the max-pairwise EO gap used in the heuristic plan, and empirical reports on MIMIC and CheXpert with 6+ demographic groups typically yield 0.08–0.18 for a standard cross-entropy baseline; 0.12 sits in the middle of that achievable band, so a well-tuned run can cross it while genuinely biased runs are flagged.
